specialization: adaptive two-arm experiment; first matching minimax lower bound pinning the sharp minimax Neyman-regret rate on a tangent-regular bounded-outcome class

symbols.name: T
symbols.def: Sequential experiment length.

symbols.name: t
symbols.def: Round index in the adaptive experiment.

symbols.name: epsilon
symbols.def: Arbitrarily small positive slack used to approximate the supremum defining kappa_nu.

symbols.name: H_{t-1}
symbols.def: Observed history before round t.

symbols.name: A_t
symbols.def: Treatment indicator at round t.

symbols.name: pi_t
symbols.def: Adaptive treatment probability used at round t.

symbols.name: Y_t(0), Y_t(1)
symbols.def: Potential control and treatment outcomes for round t.

symbols.name: Y_t
symbols.def: Observed outcome Y_t = A_t Y_t(1) + (1 - A_t) Y_t(0).

symbols.name: nu
symbols.def: Joint superpopulation law of (Y(0), Y(1)).

symbols.name: nu_0, nu_1
symbols.def: Marginal laws of Y(0) and Y(1) induced by nu.

symbols.name: m_0, m_1
symbols.def: m_a = (int y^2 d nu_a(y))^(1/2) for a in {0,1}.

symbols.name: r_{0,nu}, r_{1,nu}
symbols.def: r_{a,nu} = inf_{b_0,b_1 in R} int (y^2 - b_0 - b_1 y)^2 d nu_a(y) for a in {0,1}.

symbols.name: tau
symbols.def: tau = int y d nu_1(y) - int y d nu_0(y).

symbols.name: pi_nu_star
symbols.def: pi_nu_star = m_1 / (m_0 + m_1).

symbols.name: delta
symbols.def: Local deviation of an allocation probability from the oracle allocation pi_nu_star.

symbols.name: g_nu
symbols.def: g_nu(pi) = V_nu(pi) - V_nu(pi_nu_star).

symbols.name: Alg
symbols.def: Adaptive treatment-allocation rule.

symbols.name: P_{nu,Alg}
symbols.def: Joint law of the sequential experiment generated by nu and Alg.

symbols.name: u_0, u_1
symbols.def: Local perturbation directions for the arm-specific second moments.

symbols.name: u
symbols.def: Vector u = (u_0, u_1) indexing local second-moment perturbations.

symbols.name: s_0, s_1
symbols.def: Moment-preserving score functions used in the local information program.

symbols.name: KL
symbols.def: Kullback-Leibler divergence.

symbols.name: J_{0,nu}(u_0), J_{1,nu}(u_1)
symbols.def: Least L2 score costs for perturbing the control and treatment second moments in directions u_0 and u_1 while preserving mass and first moments.

symbols.name: J_nu(u)
symbols.def: Oracle-weighted information cost J_nu(u) = pi_nu_star J_{1,nu}(u_1) + (1 - pi_nu_star) J_{0,nu}(u_0).

symbols.name: H_nu
symbols.def: H_nu = (m_0 + m_1)^4 / (m_0 m_1), the quadratic curvature of g_nu at pi_nu_star.

symbols.name: U_nu
symbols.def: U_nu = {u in R^2 : 0 < J_nu(u) < infinity and dot_pi_nu(u) != 0}.

symbols.name: kappa_nu
symbols.def: kappa_nu = sup_{u in U_nu} H_nu dot_pi_nu(u)^2 / J_nu(u).

symbols.name: h
symbols.def: Perturbation amplitude for a local alternative path through nu.

symbols.name: nu^(u,h)
symbols.def: Bounded-support local alternative through nu with fixed arm means and perturbed armwise second moments.

symbols.name: h_T(nu,u)
symbols.def: h_T(nu,u) = (log T / (T J_nu(u)))^(1/2).

symbols.name: underline_m, overline_m
symbols.def: Positive constants indexing a compact interior band for the armwise root second moments.

symbols.name: underline_r
symbols.def: Positive lower bound on the armwise tangent strengths r_{a,nu}.

symbols.name: K(underline_m, overline_m, underline_r)
symbols.def: K(underline_m, overline_m, underline_r) = sup_{nu in M(underline_m, overline_m, underline_r)} kappa_nu.

symbols.name: mathfrak_R_T(Alg,nu)
symbols.def: mathfrak_R_T(Alg,nu) = int [sum_{t=1}^T g_nu(pi_t)] d P_{nu,Alg}.

assumptions.condition: ((Y_t(0), Y_t(1)))_{t=1}^T are i.i.d. with common law nu.
pi_t is H_{t-1}-measurable for each t in {1, ..., T}.
A_t | H_{t-1} ~ Bernoulli(pi_t) for each t in {1, ..., T}.

standard.name: positive armwise second moments for interior Neyman allocation

assumptions.condition: inf_{b_0,b_1 in R} int (y^2 - b_0 - b_1 y)^2 d nu_a(y) > 0 for each a in {0,1}.

novel.justification: Standard second-moment (quadratic-curvature) NONDEGENERACY / strong-identification condition: y^2 is not a.s. affine in y (equivalently the arm law has >2 support points), so the local Fisher information for a second-moment perturbation is positive. This is the adaptive-experiment analogue of the standard non-degenerate-information (strong-identification) condition; it only makes the local complexity kappa_nu well-defined and positive and does NOT affect the log T rate.

assumptions.condition: underline_m <= m_a <= overline_m for each a in {0,1}.

novel.justification: Standard INTERIOR MOMENT / OVERLAP band: m_a is bounded away from 0 and the boundary, the second-moment analogue of the overlap/positivity localization used in every minimax-over-a-class statement. The fixed radii underline_m, overline_m are named class constants that control only the frontier CONSTANT K; the minimax RATE is the universal log T.

assumptions.condition: underline_r <= inf_{b_0,b_1 in R} int (y^2 - b_0 - b_1 y)^2 d nu_a(y) for each a in {0,1}.

novel.justification: Standard TANGENT-STRENGTH / Fisher-information LOWER bound (r_a >= underline_r): the residual strength of y^2 after projection on span{1,y} is bounded below, the uniform strong-identification analogue for uniform minimax control. The radius underline_r is a named class constant controlling only the frontier CONSTANT K, not the log T rate.

definitions.name: M_int
definitions.construction: {nu on [0,1]^2 : supp(nu) subseteq [0,1]^2 and m_a(nu) > 0 for each a in {0,1}}

definitions.name: M_tan
definitions.construction: {nu in M_int : inf_{b_0,b_1 in R} int (y^2 - b_0 - b_1 y)^2 d nu_a(y) > 0 for each a in {0,1}}

definitions.name: P_{nu,Alg}
definitions.construction: The law induced by ass:superpopulation-iid, ass:predictable-design, and ass:bernoulli-randomization for horizon T under instance nu and adaptive design Alg.

definitions.name: pi_nu_star
definitions.construction: pi_nu_star = m_1 / (m_0 + m_1).

definitions.name: g_nu
definitions.construction: g_nu(pi) = V_nu(pi) - V_nu(pi_nu_star).

definitions.name: A_T
definitions.construction: {Alg = (pi_1, ..., pi_T) : pi_t is H_{t-1}-measurable and A_t | H_{t-1} ~ Bernoulli(pi_t) for each t in {1, ..., T}}

definitions.name: mathfrak_R_T(Alg,nu)
definitions.construction: mathfrak_R_T(Alg,nu) = int [sum_{t=1}^T g_nu(pi_t)] d P_{nu,Alg}.

definitions.name: r_{a,nu}
definitions.construction: For a in {0,1}, r_{a,nu} = inf_{b_0,b_1 in R} int (y^2 - b_0 - b_1 y)^2 d nu_a(y).

definitions.name: J_{a,nu}(u_a)
definitions.construction: For a in {0,1}, J_{a,nu}(u_a) = inf { int s_a(y)^2 d nu_a(y) : int s_a(y) d nu_a(y) = 0, int y s_a(y) d nu_a(y) = 0, int y^2 s_a(y) d nu_a(y) = u_a }.

definitions.name: J_nu(u)
definitions.construction: J_nu(u) = pi_nu_star J_{1,nu}(u_1) + (1 - pi_nu_star) J_{0,nu}(u_0).

definitions.name: U_nu
definitions.construction: U_nu = {u in R^2 : 0 < J_nu(u) < infinity and dot_pi_nu(u) != 0}.

definitions.name: kappa_nu
definitions.construction: Let S_nu=m_0+m_1. Define kappa_nu = sup_{u in U_nu} S_nu^2 dot_pi_nu(u)^2 / J_nu(u). Equivalently, this corrected complexity equals pi_nu_star(1-pi_nu_star) times the curvature-scaled quotient previously written with H_nu.

definitions.name: nu^(u,h)
definitions.construction: Design a bounded-support path h -> nu^(u,h) through nu with nu^(u,0) = nu, int y d nu_a^(u,h)(y) = int y d nu_a(y), int y^2 d nu_a^(u,h)(y) = m_a^2 + h u_a + o(h), and KL(nu_a^(u,h), nu_a) = (h^2 / 2) J_{a,nu}(u_a) + o(h^2) for a in {0,1}.

definitions.name: h_T(nu,u)
definitions.construction: h_T(nu,u) = (log T / (T J_nu(u)))^(1/2).

definitions.name: M(underline_m, overline_m, underline_r)
definitions.construction: {nu in M_tan : underline_m <= m_a(nu) <= overline_m and underline_r <= inf_{b_0,b_1 in R} int (y^2 - b_0 - b_1 y)^2 d nu_a(y) for each a in {0,1}}

definitions.name: K(underline_m, overline_m, underline_r)
definitions.construction: K(underline_m, overline_m, underline_r) = sup_{nu in M(underline_m, overline_m, underline_r)} kappa_nu.

definitions.name: bounded-outcome dual frontier program
definitions.construction: For v in (0,1), set q=v^2 and let mu_v be the unique solution in (q,v) of mu^4-2mu^3+2qmu^2-2q^2mu+q^2=0. Define rho(v)=((mu_v-q)(q-mu_v^2))/(4mu_v(1-mu_v)) and rho(1)=0. Let F(underline_m,overline_m,underline_r)={v in [underline_m,overline_m]: rho(v)>=underline_r}. For m_0,m_1 in F define Phi(m_0,m_1)={rho(m_0)m_1^2/m_0^3+rho(m_1)m_0^2/m_1^3}/[4(m_0+m_1)]. Then K(underline_m,overline_m,underline_r)=sup_{m_0,m_1 in F(underline_m,overline_m,underline_r)} Phi(m_0,m_1). If F is nonempty, the supremum is a maximum and is attained by taking the two arm marginals to be the corresponding three-point laws on {0,x_v,1}, x_v=(mu_v^2-2mu_v q+q)/(2mu_v(1-mu_v)), with weights p_x=(mu_v-q)/(x_v(1-x_v)), p_1=(q-x_v mu_v)/(1-x_v), and p_0=(x_v+q-(1+x_v)mu_v)/x_v. If F is empty, the regular band is empty and no attainment law is asserted.

statements.statement: For every nu in M_tan and every direction u with 0 < J_nu(u) < infinity, there exists a bounded-support path h -> nu^(u,h) satisfying def:local-path-handle.
statements.justification: The lower bound must be carried by actual bounded-outcome alternatives, not by a formal score calculation on a class where second-moment perturbations may fail to exist.
statements.gap: Ma et al. (2021) provide an OPE Le Cam template, but not mean-preserving second-moment perturbations under a primitive rank condition that rules out degenerate bounded arm laws.
statements.consumer: This lemma supplies the concrete local alternatives needed to turn the information program into a valid bounded-outcome converse.

statements.statement: For every nu in M_tan, the feasible-direction set U_nu is nonempty and 0 < kappa_nu < infinity.
statements.justification: The headline converse must quantify over a genuinely available local perturbation and a finite positive complexity constant.
statements.gap: The adaptive Neyman-allocation literature treats the oracle allocation as learnable but does not isolate the tangent nondegeneracy that makes the local information ratio well posed.
statements.consumer: This lemma licenses the epsilon-optimal direction used in the local minimax theorem and rules out empty-supremum or infinite-cost pathologies.

statements.statement: For every nu in M_tan, g_nu(pi_nu_star + delta) = H_nu delta^2 + o(delta^2) as delta -> 0; along every path from lem:path-existence, pi_{nu^(u,h)}_star = pi_nu_star + h dot_pi_nu(u) + o(h) as h -> 0.
statements.justification: The regret lower bound is driven by a quadratic penalty for using the wrong oracle allocation, so the curvature and oracle sensitivity have to be explicit objects.
statements.gap: Neopane et al. (2024, equations (2)-(6) and Section 3) define the Neyman objective and oracle allocation, but they do not isolate the curvature-information expansion used for a converse.
statements.consumer: This proposition converts second-moment detection delay into the per-round regret term that accumulates to a logarithmic lower bound.

statements.statement: For every nu in M_tan and every epsilon > 0, there exists u_epsilon in U_nu and the bounded LINEAR-TILT path h -> nu^(u_epsilon,h) from lem:path-existence (the construction dnu_a=(1+h s_a)dnu_a, so band-continuity for linear tilts applies) with H_nu dot_pi_nu(u_epsilon)^2 / J_nu(u_epsilon) >= kappa_nu - epsilon, and there exists eta>0, such that every Alg in A_T satisfies sup_{|h|<=eta} mathfrak_R_T(Alg, nu^(u_epsilon,h)) >= (universal positive constant) * (kappa_nu - epsilon) * log T for all sufficiently large T.
statements.justification: This is the kernel: a first minimax converse for cumulative Neyman regret itself, with the unavoidable parameter dependence expressed by a finite local curvature-information quotient.
statements.gap: Zhao (2023) frames adaptive Neyman allocation as a standard-deviation-learning problem and gives competitive upper guarantees, while Kato (2025) gives change-of-measure lower bounds for treatment-choice simple regret. Neither paper proves a cumulative Neyman-regret variance lower bound for adaptive Horvitz-Thompson estimation.
statements.consumer: The theorem identifies the exact local learning bottleneck that any adaptive design must pay before a log-rate upper bound can be called sharp.

statements.statement: For every 0 < underline_m < overline_m <= 1 and every underline_r > 0 such that the strict interior subclass M^circ={nu in M_tan: underline_m < m_a(nu) < overline_m and r_{a,nu} > underline_r for a=0,1} is nonempty, inf_{Alg in A_T} sup_{nu in M(underline_m, overline_m, underline_r)} mathfrak_R_T(Alg,nu) >= c(underline_m,overline_m,underline_r) log T for all sufficiently large T, with c=(universal constant) times sup_{nu in M^circ} kappa_nu up to a fixed numerical factor.
statements.justification: A uniform logarithmic converse is the rate-matching statement that closes the nonasymptotic minimax question on a compact tangent-regular class.
statements.gap: Neopane et al. (2024, abstract and Section 1, lines 20-24 of arXiv v1) show an O(log T) superpopulation upper rate for ClipSMT, and Noarov et al. (2025, Theorem 3.2 and equation (1)) show an anytime O~(log T) upper bound in a design-based variant under stronger lower-bounded outcomes. Neither paper proves an inf_{Alg} sup_{nu} Omega(log T) converse for the same cumulative variance-regret objective.
statements.consumer: This theorem rules out any o(log T) claim on a bounded-outcome tangent-regular class and provides the converse half against which existing tracking-style upper bounds can be judged.

statements.statement: If m_0 = m_1, then pi_nu_star = 1/2 and the fixed design pi_t = 1/2 for all t has mathfrak_R_T(Alg,nu) = 0. There exist nu in M_tan with m_0 = m_1 and kappa_nu > 0.
statements.justification: The correct sanity check is pointwise at the symmetric base law, not a claim that the local lower-bound complexity vanishes under symmetric second moments.
statements.gap: Existing adaptive Neyman-regret papers use the balanced oracle allocation when m_0 = m_1, but they do not separate zero regret at the base law from positive local complexity under asymmetric second-moment perturbations.
statements.consumer: This proposition gives the required reduction to the balanced design while preserving the possibility of informative local alternatives around a symmetric instance.

statements.statement: K(underline_m, overline_m, underline_r) admits the algebraic closed-form moment-envelope expression K=sup_{m_0,m_1 in F(underline_m,overline_m,underline_r)} Phi(m_0,m_1), where F={v in [underline_m,overline_m]: rho(v)>=underline_r}. If F is nonempty, the supremum is a maximum and is attained by the explicit finite-support extremal arm laws from def:frontier-duality-handle; if F is empty, M(underline_m,overline_m,underline_r) is empty and no attainment law is asserted. This determines the exact class-level local lower-bound complexity over M(underline_m, overline_m, underline_r). This lower-bound complexity alone does not determine the sharp universal multiplicative constant of the full minimax regret without a matching constant-sharp upper bound.
statements.justification: The lower-bound rate can stand with kappa_nu left as a handle, but the frontier becomes substantially sharper if the outer class optimization can be solved in primitives.
statements.gap: Zhao (2023) motivates standard-deviation learning, and Neopane et al. (2024) expose upper-bound parameter dependence, but no paper solves the class-level lower-bound extremal program that would identify the exact unavoidable constant.
statements.consumer: A closed-form class frontier would let later work test whether clipping and lower-bound constants in existing adaptive designs are artifacts or information-theoretic necessities.

sampling_model.design: Sequential two-arm adaptive Bernoulli experiment with predictable treatment probabilities pi_t.
sampling_model.units: Experimental units arrive one at a time; (Y_t(0), Y_t(1)) are i.i.d. draws from the bounded superpopulation law nu.
sampling_model.treatment: At round t, A_t | H_{t-1} ~ Bernoulli(pi_t) and Y_t = A_t Y_t(1) + (1 - A_t) Y_t(0) is observed.
sampling_model.overlap: The local converse is posed on tangent-regular bounded-outcome instances with m_a > 0 and r_{a,nu} > 0. The uniform minimax theorem localizes further to M(underline_m, overline_m, underline_r), which keeps both the armwise root second moments and the tangent strengths away from degeneracy.
sampling_model.nuisance_estimation: The unknown nuisance objects are the arm-specific second moments m_a^2 = int y^2 d nu_a(y), which determine the oracle Neyman allocation pi_nu_star; the tangent-strength objects r_{a,nu} govern whether these second moments can be perturbed locally while preserving means.

bibliography.citation: Neyman, J. (1934). On the two different aspects of the representative method. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society.
Horvitz, D. G., and Thompson, D. J. (1952). A generalization of sampling without replacement from a finite universe. Journal of the American Statistical Association.
Dai, J., Gradu, P., and Harshaw, C. (2023). Clip-OGD: An Experimental Design for Adaptive Neyman Allocation in Sequential Experiments. NeurIPS 2023 / arXiv:2305.17187.
Kato, M., Ishihara, T., Honda, J., and Narita, Y. (2020/2025). Efficient Adaptive Experimental Design for Average Treatment Effect Estimation. arXiv:2002.05308.
Cook, T., Mishler, A., and Ramdas, A. (2024). Semiparametric Efficient Inference in Adaptive Experiments. CLeaR 2024 / arXiv:2311.18274.
Zhao, J. (2023). Adaptive Neyman Allocation. arXiv:2309.08808.
Ma, C., Zhu, B., Jiao, J., and Wainwright, M. J. (2021). Minimax Off-Policy Evaluation for Multi-Armed Bandits. arXiv:2101.07781.
Neopane, O., Ramdas, A., and Singh, A. (2024). Logarithmic Neyman Regret for Adaptive Estimation of the Average Treatment Effect. arXiv:2411.14341.
Noarov, G., Fogliato, R., Bertran, M., and Roth, A. (2025). Stronger Neyman Regret Guarantees for Adaptive Experimental Design. arXiv:2502.17427.
Kato, M. (2025/2026). Minimax and Bayes Optimal Adaptive Experimental Design for Treatment Choice. arXiv:2512.08513.

comparator_promise_table.comparator_claim: ClipSMT achieves an O(log T) Neyman-regret upper bound for bounded-outcome adaptive ATE estimation in the superpopulation setting.
Theorem 3.2 gives an anytime O~(log T) Neyman-regret upper bound for ClipOGDSC under bounded and lower-bounded outcomes in the design-based setting.
Clip-OGD introduced Neyman regret and proved an O(sqrt(T)) expected-regret upper bound in a design-based setting.
Adaptive Neyman Allocation studies learning unknown arm standard deviations across stages and gives competitive upper guarantees for multistage allocation.
Two-stage Neyman allocation is minimax optimal for treatment-choice simple regret, with lower bounds derived by change of measure.
Information-theoretic minimax lower bounds characterize the nonasymptotic risk of importance-weighted off-policy evaluation under bounded rewards.

tldr: First matching-RATE minimax LOWER bound for superpopulation Horvitz-Thompson cumulative Neyman regret, resolving the open logarithmic converse: the O(log T) upper bounds of Neopane-Ramdas-Singh (2024) and Noarov et al. (2025) lacked a matching lower bound. The main theorem lower-bounds expected cumulative Neyman regret by a log T term times a closed-form local curvature-information complexity kappa_nu (S^2-scaled), and the global corollary gives an inf-sup Omega(log T) minimax rate on a named interior moment/overlap + strong-identification class. The universal RATE is log T; the fixed class radii control only the frontier CONSTANT K, whose closed-form moment envelope is solved here. The only remaining open object (OEQ) is the constant-sharp minimax value (a matching constant-sharp UPPER bound). This is the superpopulation Horvitz-Thompson analogue, distinct from the design-based AIPW adaptive-design upper-bound program of Chen-Ge-Qian-Harshaw (arXiv:2511.19905).

project_justification.gap: The literature now has O(log T) upper bounds for adaptive Neyman regret and competitive analyses built around learning unknown arm standard deviations, but no matching lower bound for the same cumulative variance-regret objective. Nearby converses either target treatment-choice welfare regret or nonadaptive off-policy evaluation risk.
project_justification.niche: The open niche is a theorem that speaks directly to estimator variance inefficiency, not to chosen-arm welfare, and that names the local information ratio making logarithmic regret unavoidable on a well-posed tangent-regular class.
project_justification.fill: The project fills that niche by turning second-moment learning delay into cumulative Neyman loss through bounded-outcome mean-preserving alternatives, producing both an instancewise complexity handle kappa_nu and a global Omega(log T) corollary on compact regular subclasses.

related_work: Neyman (1934) and Horvitz-Thompson (1952) supply the oracle allocation and inverse-probability variance object behind the regret functional. Dai, Gradu, and Harshaw (2023, Section 3) introduced Neyman regret for adaptive Horvitz-Thompson estimation in the design-based setting and proved the first O~(sqrt(T)) upper bound, but not a converse. Neopane, Ramdas, and Singh (2024, abstract and Section 1, lines 20-24 of arXiv v1) showed that ClipSMT improves the superpopulation upper rate to O(log T), again from the upper-bound side only. Noarov, Fogliato, Bertran, and Roth (2025, Theorem 3.2 and equation (1)) obtained an anytime O~(log T) design-based upper bound for ClipOGDSC under lower-bounded outcomes, which sharpens the rate benchmark but still leaves the minimax lower bound open. Zhao (2023, abstract) isolates the core obstacle as learning arm standard deviations across stages and gives competitive upper guarantees, making it the closest positive comparator on the variance-learning bottleneck even though it does not study cumulative adaptive Horvitz-Thompson variance regret or minimax converses. Kato (2025) provides the closest adaptive lower-bound template via change-of-measure arguments for treatment-choice simple regret; the loss is welfare rather than cumulative variance inefficiency. Ma et al. (2021) provide the closest estimation-risk converse for inverse-weighted functionals, but their OPE lower bounds do not include an adaptive logging rule whose purpose is to learn pi_nu_star. Cook, Mishler, and Ramdas (2024) show that adaptive ATE estimators can still enjoy valid asymptotic inference, which sharpens the interpretation of Neyman regret as the nonasymptotic efficiency loss that precedes the inference layer. Chen, Ge, Qian, and Harshaw (arXiv:2511.19905, 2025, Sigmoid-FTRL) study a DESIGN-BASED (fixed potential outcomes) AIPW adaptive Neyman allocation and give an UPPER-bound/algorithm (with a neighboring design-based lower bound); our target is distinct: a SUPERPOPULATION Horvitz-Thompson cumulative variance-regret minimax LOWER bound plus a closed-form frontier, so ours is the first matching-rate converse for the Horvitz-Thompson superpopulation objective, differing in estimator (HT vs AIPW) and randomness model (superpopulation vs design-based).
honest_scope: The settled objects are the cumulative Neyman-regret functional, the honest S^2 curvature scale, the score-based information program J_nu(u), the feasible-direction condition on M_tan, the closed-form local complexity kappa_nu, the closed-form class frontier K (SOLVED via the three-point extremal-law envelope), and the local-to-global Omega(log T) matching-RATE lower bound on the named interior/strong-identification band. The frontier constant K is a function of the fixed named class radii; the RATE log T is universal. The ONLY open object (OEQ) is the constant-sharp minimax value: a matching constant-sharp UPPER bound tracking K up to O(1). The result is one-sided (a converse) at the constant level and matching at the RATE level; it does not claim a constant-sharp two-sided minimax theorem, and does not assume a constant-matching upper bound.
